title: Dimensionality Reduction
description: PCA, SVD, t-SNE, UMAP, LDA, NMF, autoencoders, manifold learning, spectral embedding, feature selection for high-dimensional data visualization and preprocessing.
details: Dimensionality reduction is essential for visualizing high-dimensional data, reducing noise, mitigating the curse of dimensionality, and improving model efficiency. This file covers PCA (eigendecomposition, explained variance, whitening, probabilistic PCA), SVD and truncated SVD for sparse data (LSA), t-SNE (perplexity tuning, KL divergence, pitfalls), UMAP (Riemannian geometry, fuzzy simplicial sets, advantages over t-SNE), LDA for supervised reduction (between-class vs within-class scatter), NMF for non-negative data, autoencoders (undercomplete, sparse, denoising, variational), kernel PCA (covered in depth in Kernel Methods section), manifold learning (ISOMAP, LLE, Laplacian Eigenmaps, MDS), and feature selection (filter, wrapper, embedded, Boruta, SHAP-based). Dimensionality reduction appears in almost every ML interview — PCA and t-SNE are the most commonly tested topics.

sections.title: PCA & SVD in Depth
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: PCA finds directions of maximum variance in the data. These are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. The first principal component captures the most variance, the second captures the most remaining variance orthogonal to the first, and so on.

PCA derivation:

  • Center the data: X' = X - μ.
  • Covariance matrix: C = X'TX' / (n-1). Symmetric, PSD.
  • Eigendecomposition: C vi = λi vi. vi = principal components (loadings). λi = variance explained.
  • Projection: Z = X' V (n × k matrix of principal component scores).
  • Reconstruction: X̂ = Z VT + μ. The reconstruction error is minimized — PCA is the optimal linear dimensionality reduction in L2 sense.

SVD relationship: X' = U Σ VT. The right singular vectors V are the same as PCA eigenvectors. The singular values σi = √(n-1) λi. Using SVD directly avoids computing the covariance matrix (more numerically stable).

Choosing k (number of components):

  • Elbow rule: Plot explained variance ratio vs k, pick the elbow.
  • Threshold: Keep components until cumulative explained variance > 90% or 95%.
  • Kaiser rule: Keep eigenvectors with eigenvalues > mean eigenvalue.
  • Cross-validated reconstruction error or downstream task performance.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA, TruncatedSVD
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# === PCA from scratch ===
class PCAFromScratch:
    def __init__(self, n_components):
        self.n_components = n_components
    
    def fit(self, X):
        # Center the data
        self.mean = X.mean(axis=0)
        X_centered = X - self.mean
        
        # Covariance matrix
        cov = np.cov(X_centered, rowvar=False)
        
        # Eigendecomposition
        eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eigh(cov)
        
        # Sort in descending order
        idx = np.argsort(eigenvalues)[::-1]
        eigenvalues = eigenvalues[idx]
        eigenvectors = eigenvectors[:, idx]
        
        self.components_ = eigenvectors[:, :self.n_components]
        self.explained_variance_ = eigenvalues[:self.n_components]
        self.explained_variance_ratio_ = eigenvalues[:self.n_components] / eigenvalues.sum()
        return self
    
    def transform(self, X):
        X_centered = X - self.mean
        return np.dot(X_centered, self.components_)
    
    def inverse_transform(self, Z):
        return np.dot(Z, self.components_.T) + self.mean

# === PCA with sklearn ===
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

pca = PCA(n_components=0.95)  # Keep 95% variance
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

print(f'Original dims: {X.shape[1]}')
print(f'Reduced dims: {X_pca.shape[1]}')
print(f'Explained variance ratio: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_}')
print(f'Cumulative: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_.cumsum()}')

# === SVD for sparse data (Truncated SVD) ===
from scipy.sparse import csr_matrix

# For text data: term-document matrix is sparse
# Truncated SVD = LSA (Latent Semantic Analysis)
svd = TruncatedSVD(n_components=100, random_state=42)
X_svd = svd.fit_transform(sparse_matrix)
print(f'SVD explained variance: {svd.explained_variance_ratio_.sum():.2%}')

# === Probabilistic PCA = PCA with missing data ===
# from sklearn.decomposition import ProbabilisticPCA
# PCA assumes isotropic noise: X = WZ + mu + epsilon
# epsilon ~ N(0, sigma^2 * I)
# Can handle missing values via EM

# === Whitening ===
# Make features uncorrelated with unit variance
# Z_white = Z / sqrt(lambda)
# Useful for: preventing one component from dominating downstream model
pca_white = PCA(n_components=10, whiten=True)
X_white = pca_white.fit_transform(X_scaled)
print(f'Whitened covariance (should be identity):\n'
      f'{np.cov(X_white, rowvar=False).round(2)[:3, :3]}')

sections.title: t-SNE & UMAP for Visualization
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: t-SNE preserves local structure (nearby points in high-D stay nearby in 2D/3D). UMAP preserves both local and global structure better, is faster, and more reproducible. Both are primarily for visualization, not preprocessing.

t-SNE:

  • Step 1: Compute conditional probabilities pj|i in high-D: similarity of xj to xi under a Gaussian centered at xi.
  • Perplexity: Controls the effective number of neighbors. Typical values: 5–50. Lower = focus on local structure. Higher = global structure. Perplexity determines the bandwidth of each Gaussian.
  • Step 2: Define qij in low-D using a Student-t distribution (heavy tails prevent crowding).
  • Step 3: Minimize KL(P || Q) via gradient descent. KL divergence penalizes maps where nearby high-D points are separated in low-D (keeping clusters together). It does NOT penalize far-apart high-D points being nearby in low-D (so global structure is unreliable).
  • Pitfalls: Non-deterministic (run multiple times), sensitive to perplexity, cluster sizes are meaningless, distances between clusters are meaningless, can create false patterns in random noise.

UMAP:

  • Builds a fuzzy simplicial set representation of the data: graph where edges have probabilities between 0 and 1.
  • Optimizes low-D embedding to have similar fuzzy set cross-entropy (not just KL).
  • Advantages: 10× faster than t-SNE, better global structure preservation, deterministic (set seed), can embed new points via parametric UMAP, scales to millions of points.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# === t-SNE ===
digits = load_digits()
X, y = digits.data, digits.target

# t-SNE is stochastic — set random_state for reproducibility
tsne = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    perplexity=30,
    learning_rate='auto',
    random_state=42,
    init='pca'  # better than random init
)
X_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(X)

print(f't-SNE shape: {X_tsne.shape}')
print(f'Final KL divergence: {tsne.kl_divergence_:.2f}')
print(f'Number of iterations: {tsne.n_iter_}')

# === UMAP ===
# from umap import UMAP
# 
# umap = UMAP(
#     n_neighbors=15,        # Local vs global tradeoff (default: 15)
#     min_dist=0.1,          # Minimum distance in low-D (default: 0.1)
#     n_components=2,
#     random_state=42
# )
# X_umap = umap.fit_transform(X)
# 
# print(f'UMAP shape: {X_umap.shape}')

# === Parametric UMAP (embed new data) ===
# from umap.parametric_umap import ParametricUMAP
# 
# parametric_umap = ParametricUMAP(n_components=2, random_state=42)
# parametric_umap.fit(X_train)
# 
# # Embed new data (out-of-sample)
# X_test_umap = parametric_umap.transform(X_test)

# === t-SNE perplexity sweep (how to choose) ===
# Rule of thumb: perplexity between 5 and 50
# For n < 100: perplexity = n/4
# For n > 10000: perplexity = 30 (default is usually fine)
# Run with perplexity = [5, 15, 30, 50] and compare visually

# === Pitfalls to check ===
# 1. Run t-SNE multiple times — do clusters persist?
# 2. Check if size of clusters correlates with n_samples per class
# 3. Compare with PCA projection — are the same patterns visible?
# 4. Try on random noise: if t-SNE shows clusters, something is wrong

# === Isomap (manifold learning) ===
from sklearn.manifold import Isomap

isomap = Isomap(n_neighbors=10, n_components=2)
X_isomap = isomap.fit_transform(X)

# === MDS (Multi-Dimensional Scaling) ===
from sklearn.manifold import MDS

# Preserves pairwise distances (metric MDS)
mds = MDS(n_components=2, metric=True, random_state=42, normalized_stress='auto')
X_mds = mds.fit_transform(X)

sections.title: Feature Selection vs Feature Extraction
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Feature extraction creates new features (combinations of original). Feature selection chooses a subset of original features. Extraction is better when features are highly redundant. Selection is better when interpretability matters.

Feature selection methods:

  • Filter methods: Score each feature independently of the model. Fast but ignores feature interactions.
    • Variance threshold (remove near-constant features).
    • Mutual information (continuous target), Chi-squared (categorical).
    • F-test / ANOVA, correlation with target.
  • Wrapper methods: Use model performance to select subsets. Accounts for interactions. Slow (combinatorial).
    • Forward/Backward/Stepwise selection.
    • Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE): train model, remove least important feature, repeat.
    • Boruta: Compare feature importance to random shadow features. Keep features more important than their shadows.
  • Embedded methods: Selection happens during training.
    • L1 regularization (Lasso): forces some coefficients to zero.
    • Tree-based importance (Random Forest, XGBoost feature_importances_).
    • SHAP-based selection: remove features with consistently low SHAP values.

NMF (Non-negative Matrix Factorization): Decompose X ≈ WH where W, H ≥ 0. Useful for topic modeling, image parts, audio spectrograms. Unlike PCA, NMF learns “parts-based” representations (additive combinations of non-negative components).
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.feature_selection import (
    SelectKBest, RFE, SelectFromModel,
    mutual_info_classif, f_classif
)
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import numpy as np

# === Filter: Mutual Information ===
# MI measures non-linear dependence between X and Y
# Higher MI = more informative
selector_mi = SelectKBest(
    score_func=mutual_info_classif,
    k=100  # Keep top 100 features
)
X_mi = selector_mi.fit_transform(X, y)
mi_scores = selector_mi.scores_
selected_idx = selector_mi.get_support(indices=True)
print(f'Top 5 MI features: {selected_idx[:5]}')

# === Wrapper: Recursive Feature Elimination ===
# Uses model importance to iteratively remove features
estimator = LogisticRegression(max_iter=5000, solver='lbfgs')
rfe = RFE(estimator, n_features_to_select=50, step=10)
X_rfe = rfe.fit_transform(X, y)
print(f'RFE selected {rfe.n_features_} features')
print(f'RFE ranking (lower = better): {rfe.ranking_[:10]}')

# === Embedded: L1 regularization ===
# Lasso automatically zeros out unimportant features
selector_l1 = SelectFromModel(
    LogisticRegression(penalty='l1', C=0.1, solver='saga', max_iter=5000),
    threshold='median'
)
X_l1 = selector_l1.fit_transform(X, y)
print(f'L1 selected {X_l1.shape[1]} features')

# === Embedded: Tree importance ===
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
rf.fit(X, y)

# Select features with importance above threshold
selector_tree = SelectFromModel(rf, threshold='mean', prefit=True)
X_tree = selector_tree.transform(X)
print(f'Tree-based selection: {X_tree.shape[1]} features kept')

# === NMF for parts-based representation ===
from sklearn.decomposition import NMF

# X must be non-negative
# NMF learns: X ≈ W @ H where W, H >= 0
# W = components ("topics"), H = loadings
nmf = NMF(n_components=50, random_state=42, alpha_W=0.1, l1_ratio=0.5)
W = nmf.fit_transform(X_nonneg)  # n x 50
H = nmf.components_              # 50 x p

# Interpretation: each component is a linear combination of features
# where coefficients are non-negative (additive parts)

# === Autoencoder for non-linear DR ===
# import torch.nn as nn
# 
# class Autoencoder(nn.Module):
#     def __init__(self, input_dim, latent_dim=32):
#         super().__init__()
#         self.encoder = nn.Sequential(
#             nn.Linear(input_dim, 128), nn.ReLU(),
#             nn.Linear(128, 64),  nn.ReLU(),
#             nn.Linear(64, latent_dim)
#         )
#         self.decoder = nn.Sequential(
#             nn.Linear(latent_dim, 64), nn.ReLU(),
#             nn.Linear(64, 128),  nn.ReLU(),
#             nn.Linear(128, input_dim)
#         )
#     def forward(self, x):
#         z = self.encoder(x)
#         return self.decoder(z)

sections.title: Curse of Dimensionality & Distance Metrics
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: In high dimensions, distances between all points become nearly equal. The volume of space grows exponentially — data becomes sparse even with many points. This breaks nearest-neighbor methods, kernel methods, and distance-based clustering.

Consequences:

  • In high-D, most points are near the boundary of the space (not the center). Fraction of volume in shell: V(r) - V(r - ε) ∝ rd-1.
  • Euclidean distance concentrates: For i.i.d. features, Var[||x - x'||²] / E[||x - x'||²] → 0 as d → ∞. All pairwise distances become almost equal.
  • KNN classification fails because the “nearest neighbor” is almost as far as the “farthest neighbor.” Ratio ||xNN - x|| / ||xFN - x|| → 1 as d grows.

Mitigations:

  • Use dimensionality reduction (PCA, autoencoders) before distance-based methods.
  • Use L1 or fractional distances (they concentrate more slowly than L2).
  • Use cosine similarity instead of Euclidean distance for high-D sparse data (e.g., text).
  • Feature selection: remove irrelevant/noisy features.
  • Manifold assumption: real high-D data lies on a low-dimensional manifold. Manifold learning exploits this.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# === Demonstrate distance concentration ===
np.random.seed(42)

def distance_ratio(dim, n_points=1000):
    """Ratio of nearest to farthest point distance."""
    X = np.random.randn(n_points, dim)
    nn = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=n_points)
    nn.fit(X)
    distances, _ = nn.kneighbors(X[:1])  # distances from first point
    nearest = distances[0, 1]    # exclude self
    farthest = distances[0, -1]
    return nearest / farthest

dims = [1, 2, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000]
ratios = [distance_ratio(d) for d in dims]

for d, r in zip(dims, ratios):
    print(f'dim={d:4d}: nearest/farthest ratio = {r:.4f}')

# === Cosine similarity for high-D sparse data ===
# Cosine = (x · y) / (||x|| * ||y||)
# Range: [-1, 1]. 1 = same direction, 0 = orthogonal
# Does not suffer from concentration as badly as Euclidean

# === Fractional distances ===
# Lp norm for p < 1 concentrates more slowly
# ||x - y||_p = (sum |x_i - y_i|^p)^(1/p)
# p = 0.5 or 0.25 are sometimes used

# === Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma ===
# Any n points in high-D can be embedded into O(log n) dimensions
# while approximately preserving pairwise distances (with high probability)
# This is the theoretical justification for random projections

from sklearn.random_projection import GaussianRandomProjection

# Random projection: preserve distances with JL lemma
rp = GaussianRandomProjection(n_components='auto', eps=0.1, random_state=42)
# 'auto' = min dims to preserve pairwise distances with eps tolerance
print(f'JL-recommended dims: {rp.n_components}')
X_rp = rp.fit_transform(X)

# === Hubness phenomenon ===
# In high dimensions, some points become "hubs" (appear as nearest
# neighbor of many other points) while others are "anti-hubs" (never
# nearest neighbor to anyone). This breaks KNN.
# Mitigation: use shared-neighbor distances or mutual proximity
